entry.title: Re: Contingent Worker/External to Employee Conversions th...
entry.content: This is not a viable solution for us, and likely not for others as well. Our contingent workers typically end their assignment on Friday and return on Monday as employees. We enter the contingent worker termination on Sunday to ensure they do not lose IT access during the transition to employee status. Even if we were to terminate them on Saturday, that would leave only one day for HR to initiate onboarding, for the contingent worker to complete their tasks, and for HR to finalize onboarding as a new hire. During this gap, the worker would lose access and it's not feasible to complete all tasks in one day. In Onboarding 1.0, the system recognized future‑dated terminations and allowed workers to proceed with onboarding using a future hire date without requiring termination. Why was this functionality not considered in Onboarding 2.0? What was previously an automated process has now become highly manual.

entry.title: Re: Contingent Worker/External to Employee Conversions th...
entry.content:
@tracymca 

This is not a viable solution for us, and likely not for others as well. Our contingent workers typically end their assignment on Friday and return on Monday as employees. We enter the contingent worker termination on Sunday to ensure they do not lose IT access during the transition to employee status. It is business critical they do not lose access.

Even if we were to terminate them on Saturday, that would leave only one day for HR to initiate onboarding, for the contingent worker to complete their tasks, and for HR to finalize onboarding as a new hire. During this gap, the worker would lose access and it's not feasible to complete all tasks in one day.

In Onboarding 1.0, the system recognized future‑dated terminations and allowed workers to proceed with onboarding using a future termination date as long as the hire date was after the termination date.  Why was this functionality not considered in Onboarding 2.0?

What was previously an automated process has now become highly manual.

entry.title: Why ABAP do not have JSON de/serializer in kernel?
entry.content:
And with JSON I mean 'canonical' default JSON as per JavaScript, which widely spread and supported in many languages. And not asJSON (ABAP Serialization JSON) which is supported by transformations in ABAP. 

All other de/serializations written in ABAP, like /ui2/cl_json, new one in cloud xco_json, and many open source ones are very slow, and cannot be fast as written in ABAP.

I summarized my idea in improvement request https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#idea/367772, if you think it is relevant for you too, please vote.

entry.title: Re: Delete F4579 configuration
entry.content:
Hello,
BC maintenance objects (R3TR SMBC) can be deleted like any other repository object in ADT.
Just removing the TADIR entry does not delete the object from the relevant table SMBC_CONFIG/SMBC_CONFIG_TEXT. Instead, you can't work with the object anymore. The F4579 will still show it because the relevant CDS view does not care about TADIR, only SMBC_CONFIG. I suggest you restore the TADIR entry and the delete it in ADT.
Regards,
Patrick

entry.title: Re: Central Finance Tips and Tricks #5 - SLT
entry.content:
Hi @MarcE 
I have a ongoing issue in SLT. Do you have any idea how to resolve this?

Issue: Filters (Conversion logic) not working.

Scenario:
We have 3 non-SAP Source systems from which the data is being extracted and transformed by a 3rd party ETL tool and then pushed into SLT system and the expectation is that the data would flow onto the CFIN system. However, we have only 2 environments for SLT system (Pre-PROD & PROD). So, the idea is to create 2 Mass Transfer IDs and put Filters (for both Initial Load & Ongoing Replication) to be able to segregate data from Source (DEV) & Source (QAS) and then push it to respective Target CFIN (DEV) & CFIN (QAS) systems. The filters should ensure that Source DEV data gets pushed to Target Dev and Source QAS data gets pushed to Target QAS.

Steps Followed:
1. Created 3 RFCs
A. Pointing from SLT to SLT (RFC_SLT)
B. Pointing from SLT to CFIN DEV (RFC_CFIN_D)
C. Pointing from SLT to CFIN QAS (RFC_CFIN_Q)

2. Created 2 MTID
A. MTID_SLT_CFIN_D [Used RFC_SLT in Source Settings & RFC_CFIN_D in target settings].
B. MTID_SLT_CFIN_Q [Used RFC_SLT in Source Settings & RFC_CFIN_Q in target settings].

3. Create Predefined Load / Replication Objects
A. For Load Object:
Copied from Std. Repl Object (Project: REPL_CFIN, Subproject: REPL_CFIN_ST, Predefined Object: CFIST_ACDOC_L) and used Table /1LT/CF_E_HEADER.
B. For Replication Object:
Copied from Std. Repl Object (Project: REPL_CFIN, Subproject: REPL_CFIN_ST, Predefined Object: CFIST_ACDOC_R) and used Table /1LT/CF_E_HEADER.

4. Add Conversion Logic to Subproject.
Selected Subproject and then created a Conversion Logic by changing the Event-based Rule: SKIP_DOCUMENT. [Logic: If Logical System = SDEV then SKIP_RECORD, SDEV - Logical system for Source DEV system].

5. Generated Runtime Object by completing all the Steps for Runtime Object for Load Object.

6. Attempted to generate Runtime object for Replication Object (but FAILED with error stating that a undefined structure <IT_S> exists). On checking the Function Group generated after attempting to generate runtime objects, this structure exists and is truly undefined.

7. Advance Replication (LTRS) Settings:
Applied Filter Logic [Logical system = SDEV]

We have not setup the MTID for QAS system yet. However, when data is sent for Source DEV & Source QAS to test the FILTERS, data from both the Source system environments get transferred to CFIN DEV which shows that the FILTERS ARE NOT WORKING.

entry.title: Re: How to open cases for end customer as an SAP Partner
entry.content:
Hello,

You may find the "Service Partner User" (SPU) function useful for your issue.

As a partner, you can refer to KBA 2518736 - "Service Partners: How to get authorizations for your S-user ID to create support cases on-behalf of your customers - SAP for Me".

Your customer needs to invite your S-user as written in KBA 2518600 - "How does a customer invite partner S-user IDs to create cases on their behalf using the Service Partner User functionality - SAP for Me".
(It's important to pay special attention to step 6. "Set a validity period for their engagement" or otherwise the functionality will not work.)

If you need any further assistance, you may contact SAP Partner Support as per KBA 1848459 - How do SAP Partners get help with all partner topics and applications (including Partner Dashboards in SAP for Me)

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End
entry.content:
One of the most powerful capabilities in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) is its ability to automate invoice processing the moment a customer or vendor sends an email. With SAP Document Information Extraction (Document AI) integrated into ESM, the journey from a raw invoice PDF to a validated posting in SAP S/4HANA Private or Public Edition becomes almost fully automated.
 

Step 1: The User Sends an Email with an Invoice

A customer or vendor sends an email to the SAP ESM dedicated email address configured for automatic case creation.

The invoice may be:

  • Attached as a PDF, JPEG, PNG, or
  • Embedded in the email body itself

SAP ESM picks up both.

Step 2: SAP ESM Automatically Creates a Case

Once the email reaches the ESM mailbox:

  • A new case is created automatically
  • The email body and all attachments are stored within the case
  • The attachments (PDFs, images) trigger SAP Document AI processing immediately

This eliminates manual case creation entirely.

Step 3: SAP Document AI Processes the Invoice

SAP Document AI retrieves the attachment from the case and begins extraction using OCR and machine learning.

It identifies:

  • Invoice number
  • Customer or vendor name
  • Address
  • Line items
  • Totals & taxes
  • Payment terms
  • Contract or PO references

Document AI also calculates a confidence score for each extracted field.
These scores help determine whether the data is reliable enough to auto‑post or requires user intervention.

Step 4: Validation Using Business Rules & Customer Framework

Once extraction is complete, SAP ESM applies:

  • Business rules
  • Customer validation frameworks
  • Mapping logic
  • Master data checks

These validations are essential before communicating with S/4HANA.

Examples:

  • Is the customer known in S/4?
  • Is the invoice number valid and not duplicated?
  • Does the PO exist?
  • Do line item totals match?

Only when the validations pass does ESM proceed to the next step.

Step 5: Invoice Number & Customer Auto‑Identification

If Document AI finds the invoice number, SAP ESM updates the line items and case fields:

  • invoiceNumber → updated
  • customerNumber → auto‑identified
  • isClearing → set according to logic (e.g., invoice found, ready to post)

This gives the ESM user immediate visibility:

  • Has the system recognized the invoice?
  • Has the customer been identified?
  • Is the invoice ready for posting?

Step 6: ESM Sends the Validated Invoice to S/4HANA via OData APIs

Once the case has all mandatory and validated data, the ESM user can trigger the next action:

“Post Invoice to S/4”

At this point:

  • ESM calls S/4HANA OData APIs
  • A draft or final FI invoice is created
  • Additional steps like clearing, matching, or workflow approvals occur inside S/4

This hands-off process ensures:

  • No manual entry in S/4
  • No data inconsistencies
  • Full auditability from source → document → posting

Step 7: ESM User Monitors and Completes the Process

Inside the ESM case, the user can now see:

  • Status of the posting
  • Document number returned from S/4
  • Any errors (e.g., master data inconsistencies)
  • New actions available for follow-up

They can then:

  • Update the customer
  • Add missing reference information
  • Re-trigger the posting
  • Close the case once processing is complete

Result: A Fully Streamlined, AI‑Driven Invoice Workflow

By integrating SAP ESM, Document AI, and S/4HANA, organizations benefit from:

✔ Zero manual invoice entry

✔ Auto-identification of customers and invoice data

✔ Early error detection using business rules

✔ Faster posting to S/4HANA

✔ End-to-end transparency in one central case

✔ Higher accuracy and reduced effort

This modernizes invoice handling from a traditionally manual process into an intelligent, automated workflow.

Learning Journey :  Business Documents in ESM

entry.title: Re: change key fields in custom BO in SAP C4C V1 after tr...
entry.content:
Modifying Alternative Key Fields in SAP C4C Custom Business Objects After Transport
Current Situation Analysis
Based on your CBO definition, you want to remove the [AlternativeKey] annotation from field2 after the business object has already been transported to multiple tenants. This is a significant structural change that requires careful consideration.

Key Limitations for Transported CBOs
Critical Restriction: You cannot modify or delete fields that were transported [1]. This is implemented by design to prevent data loss and maintain system integrity across tenants ,[2].

Specific Limitations Include:

Cannot change field types of transported fields
Cannot modify key field attributes (add/remove AlternativeKey annotation) [3],[2],[4]
Cannot delete transported fields [1],[5]
Cannot change field IDs [6]
Assessment: Regular Patch vs Deletion Patch
Regular Patch Transport: Not Supported

Removing the [AlternativeKey] annotation from field2 constitutes a structural change to the key definition
This type of modification is not allowed through regular patch transport for transported CBOs [1],[2]
Deletion Patch: Required but Limited

A deletion patch would be necessary for this type of structural change [7]
However, deletion patches are primarily designed for removing entire fields or objects, not modifying annotations
Even with deletion patches, changing key field attributes may not be supported
Recommended Solutions
Option 1: Create New Custom Business Object (Recommended)
Process:

Copy the existing CBO using the "Copy" function in the Custom Business Objects app [8],[2]
Modify the new CBO by removing the [AlternativeKey] annotation from field2
Update all dependencies:
Custom Logic references
UI elements
Integration points
Reports and queries
Migrate data from old CBO to new CBO (if needed)
Deactivate the old CBO by removing all UI access and business logic references [2]
Option 2: Add New Field as Alternative Key
Alternative Approach:

Add a new field (e.g., field5) with the [AlternativeKey] annotation
Keep field2 as is (since it cannot be modified)
Update business logic to use the new alternative key field
Gradually phase out usage of field2 as a key in custom logic
Implementation Steps for Option 1
Dependency Analysis

Navigate to Extensibility Inventory from your CBO to check all dependencies [2],[4]
Document all objects that reference the current CBO
Create New CBO

import AP.Common.GDT as apCommonGDT;

businessobject CBO_V2 {
[Label("field1")] [AlternativeKey] element SerialNumber : ID ;
[Label("field2")] element field2 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description; // AlternativeKey removed
[Label("field3")] element field3 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
[Label("field4")] element field4 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
}
Update Dependencies

Modify all custom logic to reference the new CBO
Update UI elements and forms
Adjust integration scenarios
Data Migration (if required)

Use OData services to extract data from old CBO [9]
Load data into new CBO structure
Risk Mitigation Strategies
Data Preservation:

The old CBO will remain in the system but should be made inaccessible to end users [2]
All business data remains intact in the original structure
Testing Protocol:

Thoroughly test the new CBO in development system
Validate all business processes using the new structure
Ensure query performance meets requirements
Rollback Plan:

Keep the original CBO structure as backup
Document all changes for potential rollback scenarios
Best Practices for Future CBO Design
Alternative Key Planning:

Carefully consider alternative key requirements before initial transport [10]
Design key structures that are unlikely to change
Use UUID-based keys when human-readable keys might change
Transport Strategy:

Plan structural changes before initial transport
Consider creating multiple versions of CBOs for different use cases
Document key field dependencies thoroughly
Conclusion
Unfortunately, there is no direct way to modify the AlternativeKey annotation of field2 in your transported CBO. The recommended approach is to create a new CBO with the desired structure and migrate your business processes to use the new object. While this requires additional effort, it ensures data integrity and system stability across all your tenants.

entry.title: Re: Group Reporting 2023 On-Prem: Due to the latest upgra...
entry.content:
Please check SAP for Me and also on this Community site, a number of items are dedicated to  S/4HANA and "Group Reporting" issues when upgrading.

KBA 3446422 - Group Reporting: How to check What's New section in Group Reporting - SAP for Me indicates how / why you should inspect the "What's New" section carefully of a new S/4-version ... Next, see KBA 2371973 - EC-CS in the context of SAP S/4HANA OP ( "on premise" ) - SAP for Me and the following extract related to S/4 2023 OP :

EC-CS in SAP S/4HANA OP 2023

  • For usage of EC-CS in S/4HANA OP 2023, software component S4CORE, release level 108 is required.
  • Mainstream maintenance of EC-CS in SAP S/4HANA OP 2023 will be terminated with the compatibility scope, at end of year 2025.
  • If you are using EC-CS in SAP S/4HANA, you need to migrate to the alternative functionality SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting. See SAP Note 2833748 for details. The license for SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting includes usage right for EC-CS until December 31st, 2027.

The information of KBA 2833748 - SAP S/4HANA OP ( "on premise" ) - Transition from EC-CS to SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting - SAP for Me is extensive and should be analyzed on missing steps when upgrading ...

Finally, KBA 3640892 - Group Reporting: Error pop-up in Data Monitor. System has been upgraded from release S/4HANA 1909. - SAP for Me refers to S/4 1909 but is good as a Solution Proposal. If the mentioned Config does not match with your S/4 2023 OP System, I would suggest referencing the KBA 3640892 in a Support Case to SAP, suggesting to create a similar Guide for S/4 2023 ...

Hope this helps

Nic T.

entry.title: I need a process for bulk liquid deliveries with multiple stops per truck.
entry.content: My company will ship a tank truck of product ~5000 gallons or 40000 Lbs. and will stop off at multiple ship to locations e.g. 400 gallons 1st, then 500 gallons 2nd, and so on. We would like to post goods issue the billable qty as per stop off for what they received. Is there a best practice for this request? We are currently creating a delivery and confirm pick quantity as actual and change the sales order to match the delivery quantity then we bill the customer based on each drop's actual quantity. This being a manual process I would like to see if there is a standard process for this scenario.

entry.title: From Business Objects to Business Flows: The Missing Layer of RAP Documentation
entry.content:
Part 2 in a series on modern SAP documentation.

Part 1 covered Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices. This post tackles what happens when you need to understand how it all fits together.

In my last post, I argued that RICEFW documentation doesn't fit how we build with RAP. Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices give us a better way to document individual capabilities and the decisions behind them.

But I ended with an open question: what happens when someone asks “Walk me through what happens when a customer creates an order”? You point them to four BO Canvases. They read all four. They still don't understand the sequence — what triggers what, in which order, and what happens when something fails.

Functional Specs Describe Pieces, Not Flows

Our team documents in Confluence today. Functional specifications per RICEFW object — one for the report, another for the interface, another for the enhancement, another for the form. They're organized around technical artifacts, not business capabilities.

So if you want to understand the Sales Order, you're reading across half a dozen specs. The enhancement spec for validation, the interface spec for pricing, the form spec for order confirmation. Each makes sense on its own. But piecing together what actually happens when a customer creates an order means jumping between documents that were never designed to tell that story.

Questions like these are hard to answer:

What happens after the sales order is saved? What events fire, and in what order? If credit check fails, does the order stay blocked, or does it roll back? Which steps are synchronous and which are async? Where does pricing fit — before, during, or after save?

The answers live in people's heads, scattered across specs, or buried in code.

Why RAP Makes This Worse — and Better

In classic ABAP, process orchestration was often implicit. User exits, BAdIs, and function modules wired through transaction codes. You could trace the flow by reading the code.

RAP changes this. Business objects are decoupled. They communicate through events, EML calls, and service projections. Good architecture — but the process flow is no longer visible in any single place. The good news: because RAP forces you to think in events and clear BO boundaries, the sequence can be documented cleanly. You just need to actually do it.

An Idea: Sequence Maps

What I think we need is something simple — a visual flow that shows the chain of BOs in a business process, the trigger between each step, and whether it's synchronous or asynchronous. Nothing fancy. A diagram in Confluence: Customer creates order → Sales Order BO (SAVE) → [sync] Pricing determination → [sync] Credit check → [event] OrderCreated → [async] Inventory reservation → [async] Shipping notification

Even something this basic should be valuable. New developers could see the flow in thirty seconds. Architects could spot the sync/async boundaries. Application experts could validate whether the sequence matches what they expect.

The Full Picture

Each documentation layer answers a different question:

  • BO Canvas: What does this business object do?
  • ADR: Why did we build it this way?
  • Dependency matrix: What's connected to what?
  • Sequence map: What happens when a business process runs?

The sequence mapping is the part I haven't started yet — but it's the gap I feel most in daily work. Too many “go ask Henrik, he knows” conversations. If you're on a similar journey — moving from RICEFW specs to something that fits RAP — I'd like to hear what you've tried.

entry.title: Replicating ASE TDE to heterogenous target (Oracle)
entry.content:
Hi - 

SAP documentation is a bit confusing when discussing replicating encrypted data to a heterogenous target (Oracle).  

Encrypted columns says no heterogenous targets are supported but other documentation may indicate support TDE ASE replication to a heterogenous target.

entry.title: is it possible to delete a sap user from 000 client by logging in from 100 client
Re: CCV2 no column name was specified for column 2 of CNT...
entry.content:
The paginated FlexibleSearch wraps your query into a derived table (the CNTTABLEALIAS you see in the exception) to compute counts / apply pagination. In that derived table, every selected column must have a name.
Your select query:

SELECT item_t0.p_customer , MAX( item_t0.createdTS )

Column #2 is an expression without an alias, so the derived table ends up with “column 2 has no name” → exactly the error:

No column name was specified for column 2 of 'CNTTABLEALIAS'

Fix: alias the 2nd selected column in the outer SELECT

Give the MAX(...) (or any column expression) an alias, e.g. AS maxColumn

entry.title: Re: Replicating ASE TDE to heterogenous target (Oracle)
entry.content:
In further reading it sounds like this is seemless w/in ASE so no impact to users including replication to any targets.  Encrypted column replication is not supported to heterogenous targets.

Full Database Encryption

SAP ASE version 16.0 introduces the ability to encrypt entire databases, providing protection for an entire database without affecting existing applications.

Once you encrypt a database, all of its data, indexes, and transaction logs become encrypted. This encryption is transparent, so that users can perform operations on tables, indexes, and so on, as usual, without noticing any differences.

entry.title: Custom Handlers for Entities in SAP CAP
entry.content:
The first time I faced a two-level composition hierarchy with draft-enabled entities in a CAP project, I stared at my screen wondering which custom handlers I actually needed to implement. The Fiori Elements UI works beautifully out of the box—users can create, edit, save, and discard changes without any custom code. But the moment you need business logic, validations, or calculations, you realize you’re dancing with an intricate system of events that needs careful choreography. Nowadays, while shifting from one project to another or after sometime without 'coding'. I'm wondering which  is the correct one that I have to use. For that reason I created this blog post to helps me surf between the events and their lifecycle in draft entities.

In this post, I’ll walk through each Fiori Elements action and explain exactly what custom handlers you need to implement for a draft-enabled entity with nested compositions. My mind loves finding patterns, and CAP’s draft system is essentially a beautiful pattern that becomes clearer once you understand the rhythm.

Understanding the Scenario

Let’s establish our data model first. We have:

  • Entity A: Draft-enabled parent entity
  • Entity B: Composition of Entity A (automatically draft-enabled)

Here’s the critical insight: when a parent entity is draft-enabled, all compositions in the hierarchy automatically inherit draft behavior. There’s no such thing as mixing draft and non-draft entities in a composition tree—CAP handles the entire hierarchy as one cohesive draft unit.

This means when a user edits Entity A, CAP creates draft copies of all related Entity B records. When they activate (save) the draft, all changes across the hierarchy are committed atomically. This transactional behavior is what makes the draft system powerful, but it also means your custom handlers need to respect this hierarchy.

Entity A (draft-enabled)
  └── Entity B[] (composition, inherits draft)

The Fiori Elements Action Lifecycle

When users interact with a Fiori Elements application, their actions trigger specific CAP events. Let’s break down each action, the events it triggers, and the custom handlers you need.

Quick Reference Matrix

User ActionCAP EventTarget EntityEntities AffectedWhen To use
Create (New)NEWEntityA.drafts, EntityB.draftsEntityA draft; EntityB when adding a child rowSet defaults, initialize structure
Edit (Existing)EDITEntityAAll entities (A, B) - active → draft copyValidate edit allowed, enrich draft context
Update (Modify fields)PATCHEntityA.drafts, EntityB.draftsSpecific draft entity being changedField validation, calculations, cascade updates
Save/Activate

SAVE then 

CREATE/UPDATE

EntityA.drafts → EntityAAll entities (draft → active)Critical: Validate all entities, business logic
DeleteDELETE

EntityA or EntityA.drafts; 

EntityB.drafts for individual child rows

All entities via cascade (root delete); specific child rowPrevent deletion, cleanup resources
Cancel/DiscardDISCARDEntityA.draftsAll draft entities deletedCleanup temp resources

Important Notes:

  • SAVE is special: It triggers BOTH a SAVE event on drafts AND either CREATE (for new entities) or UPDATE (for edited entities) on the active entity. You need handlers for all three.
  • Compositions inherit draft: When EntityA is draft-enabled, EntityB automatically becomes draft-enabled. No way to mix draft/non-draft in a composition tree.
  • Deep operations: CAP handles the entire hierarchy automatically during EDIT and SAVE. Trust it unless you have specific needs.
  • Use .drafts qualifier: Always distinguish between EntityA (active) and EntityA.drafts (draft) in your handlers.

Action 1: NEW (Creating a New Draft)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Create” button in the list report, or adds a new row to a composition child table
  • CAP Event: NEW on EntityA.drafts (new parent draft) or NEW on EntityB.drafts (new child row added during editing)
  • Entities Affected: EntityA draft when creating; EntityB draft when the user adds a row to the composition table
  • When You Need a Handler: To set default values, initialize related entities, or prepare the initial structure
import { Request } from '@sap/cds';

// Fires when the user clicks "Create" in the list report
srv.before('NEW', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Set default values for the new draft
  req.data.status = 'NEW';
  req.data.createdBy = req.user.id;
});

srv.after('NEW', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Enrich the created draft with calculated fields
  // or fetch additional data to display
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: Calculate some initial values based on user context
  await UPDATE('EntityA.drafts')
    .set({ calculatedField: someCalculation() })
    .where({ ID });
});

// Fires when the user adds a new row to the EntityB composition table
// This is independent from the parent NEW — it fires on the child entity directly
srv.before('NEW', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Set defaults for the new child row
  req.data.position = await getNextPosition(req.data.parent_ID);
  req.data.lineStatus = 'OPEN';
});

srv.after('NEW', 'EntityB.drafts', async (data: EntityB, req: Request) => {
  // Post-process the newly created child row
  const { ID } = data;
  await UPDATE('EntityB.drafts')
    .set({ computedField: computeDefault(data) })
    .where({ ID });
});

Action 2: EDIT (Editing an Active Entity)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Edit” button on an existing record
  • CAP Event: EDIT action on EntityA, which internally triggers draft creation
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy (active → draft copy)
  • When You Need a Handler: To customize the draft preparation, load additional context, or handle special composition copying logic
srv.before('EDIT', 'EntityA', async (req: Request) => {
  // Fetch the active entity before it's copied to draft
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const entity = await SELECT.one.from('EntityA').where({ ID });

  // You can perform validations before allowing edit
  if (entity.locked) {
    req.error(423, 'This entity is locked and cannot be edited');
  }
});

srv.after('EDIT', 'EntityA', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // The draft has been created with deep copy of all compositions
  // You can now enrich the draft with additional data
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: Load some context data into the draft
  await UPDATE('EntityA.drafts')
    .set({ editContext: 'User edited at ' + new Date().toISOString() })
    .where({ ID });

  // CAP automatically handles the deep copy of EntityB
  // You typically don't need to manually copy compositions
});

Action 3: PATCH (Modifying a Draft)

  • User Action: Changes field values while editing (each field change triggers this)
  • CAP Event: PATCH on EntityA.drafts or EntityB.drafts
  • Entities Affected: The specific draft entity being modified
  • When You Need a Handler: For field-level validation, calculated fields, or cascading updates
srv.before('PATCH', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate the changes before they're applied
  const { amount, currency } = req.data;

  if (amount && amount < 0) {
    req.error(400, 'Amount must be positive');
  }

  // Perform calculations based on changed fields
  if (req.data.quantity && req.data.pricePerUnit) {
    req.data.totalPrice = req.data.quantity * req.data.pricePerUnit;
  }
});

srv.after('PATCH', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Cascade updates to child entities if needed
  const { ID } = data;

  // Example: When parent changes, update all children
  if (req.data.status) {
    await UPDATE('EntityB.drafts')
      .set({ parentStatus: req.data.status })
      .where({ parent_ID: ID });
  }
});

// Handle updates on child entities
srv.before('PATCH', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate child entity changes
  // These fire independently when users edit composition tables
  if (req.data.invalidField) {
    req.error(400, 'Invalid field value');
  }
});

Action 4: SAVE/Activate (Persisting Draft Changes)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Save” button
  • CAP Event: SAVE on EntityA.drafts, which triggers either CREATE or UPDATE on the active EntityA
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy (draft → active)
  • When You Need a Handler: This is where most business logic lives—validation, computation, and composition management
// IMPORTANT: The SAVE handler runs on the draft entity
srv.before('SAVE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // This is your last chance to validate before activation
  const draft = await SELECT.one
    .from('EntityA.drafts')
    .where({ ID: req.data.ID })
    .columns(['ID', 'requiredField', 'status']);

  if (!draft.requiredField) {
    req.error(400, 'Required field is missing');
  }

  // Validate all child entities in the composition
  const childrenB: EntityB[] = await SELECT.from('EntityB.drafts')
    .where({ parent_ID: draft.ID });

  for (const childB of childrenB) {
    if (!childB.mandatoryField) {
      req.error(400, `EntityB ${childB.ID} is missing mandatory field`);
    }
  }
});

// Handle new entity creation (when activating a draft created via "Create")
srv.on('CREATE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request, next: Function) => {
  // The draft is being activated and will become a new active entity
  // CAP handles the deep insert of all compositions automatically

  // But you might need to customize the creation
  const result = await next(); // Let CAP do the default deep insert

  // Post-creation logic
  await postProcessNewEntity(result.ID);

  return result;
});

// Handle entity update (when activating a draft created via "Edit")
srv.on('UPDATE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request, next: Function) => {
  // The draft changes are being applied to the active entity
  // CAP handles the deep update of all compositions

  // You can intercept to add custom logic
  const { ID } = req.data;

  // CAP's deep update uses a "full set" approach:
  // - Existing child records not in the draft are deleted
  // - New child records in the draft are created
  // - Modified child records are updated

  const result = await next(); // Let CAP handle the deep update

  // Post-update logic
  await notifyRelatedSystems(ID);

  return result;
});

srv.after('SAVE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // The draft has been successfully activated
  // This fires AFTER the CREATE or UPDATE completes

  // Good place for notifications, logging, or async processes
  await sendNotification({
    message: 'Entity A has been saved',
    entityId: data.ID
  });
});

Action 5: DELETE (Removing an Entity)

  • User Action: Deletes a draft or active record
  • CAP Event: DELETE on EntityA or EntityA.drafts
  • Entities Affected: All entities in the hierarchy via cascade deletion
  • When You Need a Handler: To prevent deletion under certain conditions, clean up related data, or handle cascade manually
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityA', async (req: Request) => {
  // Validate deletion is allowed
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const entity = await SELECT.one.from('EntityA').where({ ID });

  if (entity.status === 'LOCKED') {
    req.error(403, 'Cannot delete locked entities');
  }

  // CAP will automatically cascade delete to EntityB
  // due to the composition relationship

  // But you might want to clean up related data outside the composition
  await deleteRelatedResources(ID);
});

srv.after('DELETE', 'EntityA', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Entity and all compositions have been deleted
  // Clean up, log, or notify
  await logDeletion(data.ID, req.user.id);
});

// Deleting the entire draft (when user deletes the whole unsaved record)
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Usually no custom logic needed here
  // CAP handles cascade deletion of draft compositions
  // But you might want to log or clean up
});

// Fires when the user removes an individual row from the EntityB composition table
// This is different from deleting the whole parent draft — it targets a specific child row
srv.before('DELETE', 'EntityB.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  const { ID } = req.data;
  const item = await SELECT.one.from('EntityB.drafts').where({ ID });

  if (item.protected) {
    req.error(403, `Item ${item.ID} cannot be removed`);
  }
});

srv.after('DELETE', 'EntityB.drafts', async (data: EntityB, req: Request) => {
  // Child row has been removed from the draft composition
  // You might want to recalculate totals or reorder positions on the parent draft
  await recalculateTotals(data.parent_ID);
});

Action 6: CANCEL (Discarding Draft Changes)

  • User Action: Clicks the “Cancel” or “Discard Draft” button
  • CAP Event: Draft discard action
  • Entities Affected: All draft entities in the hierarchy are deleted
  • When You Need a Handler: To clean up temporary data or resources created during draft editing
srv.before('DISCARD', 'EntityA.drafts', async (req: Request) => {
  // Clean up any temporary resources or cached data
  const { ID } = req.data;

  // Example: Delete temporary files uploaded to the draft
  await cleanupTemporaryFiles(ID);

  // CAP will automatically delete all draft entities in the composition
});

srv.after('DISCARD', 'EntityA.drafts', async (data: EntityA, req: Request) => {
  // Draft has been discarded
  // Log the discard action if needed
  await logDraftDiscard(data.ID, req.user.id);
});

Key Patterns & Best Practices

After working with draft-enabled compositions, here are the patterns I’ve found most useful:

1. Use the .drafts qualifier consistently: Always be explicit about whether you’re handling drafts or active entities. EntityA.drafts vs EntityA are different entities with different behavior.

2. Handle both CREATE and UPDATE during SAVE: When a draft is activated, it triggers either CREATE (new entity) or UPDATE (edited entity). You need handlers for both scenarios—they’re not interchangeable.

3. Trust CAP’s deep operations: CAP automatically handles deep INSERT and UPDATE for your entire composition hierarchy. Don’t try to manually manage child entities during activation unless you have specific requirements.

4. Validate in before(‘SAVE’): The SAVE event is your last checkpoint before draft activation. Put comprehensive validation here, including validation of child and grandchild entities.

5. Manage transactions carefully: All operations during draft activation are transactional. If you throw an error or call req.error(), the entire activation (including all compositions) will roll back.

6. Use after handlers for side effects: Send notifications, update external systems, or trigger async processes in after handlers. These run only after successful completion, ensuring consistency.

 

Rules for AI Code Assistants (CAP Draft)

Use this a template for your rules.

# SAP CAP Draft Handler Events

## Draft root entity (EntityA.drafts)
- Create new draft    → NEW       (not CREATE)
- Edit field in draft → PATCH     (not UPDATE)
- Save/activate draft → SAVE → then CREATE (new) or UPDATE (edit) on active entity
- Edit existing       → EDIT      on active entity (not drafts)
- Discard draft       → DISCARD   (not discard)
- Delete              → DELETE    on EntityA or EntityA.drafts

## Non-draft entities — standard CRUD only
- CREATE / READ / UPDATE / DELETE on the entity directly
- No .drafts qualifier, no NEW/PATCH/SAVE/EDIT/DISCARD

## Key rules
- Always use .drafts qualifier to distinguish draft from active entity
- SAVE handlers go on EntityA.drafts; activate handlers on active EntityA
- before('SAVE') is the last validation checkpoint before activation
- after() handlers are for side effects (notifications, external calls)
- All activation operations are transactional; req.error() rolls back everything

In case that  the files are very big think to add as an external reference as: 

## To choose event and target entity for a Custom Handler for entity check `<relative_project_root>/<code_assistant>/docs/cap_handlers.md`

This documentation is based in amazing job in https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/fiori

entry.title: Customer Invoices based on batches from Outbound delivery - Public cloud
entry.content:
Hi,

We have a business requirement (Public Cloud) where we have to create invoices based on the batches from the outbound delivery.

For example, if we have 3 batches for a line item in the outbound delivery. When we want to invoice, it is required to create the first invoice only for first 2 batches and later second invoice to be created for the 3rd batch. User will decide which batch to be invoiced.

Do we have any solution for this requirement? User don't want to create one line item per batch in the outbound delivery as the number of batches will be high for a line item.

Thanks in advance.

With Regards

Vinu Nanjan

entry.title: Re: Bienvenidos al grupo de SAP Community Mexico
entry.content:
¡Hola a todos! 👋 Es un gran gusto unirme a esta comunidad. Les comparto un poco sobre mi perfil y lo que busco construir con ustedes:

¿En qué parte de México te encuentras o planeas visitar?
Radico en la CDMX  y actualmente soy estudiante de Ingeniería en Sistemas en la ESCOM del IPN.

¿En qué área o módulo de SAP estás trabajando actualmente?
En este momento estoy enfocado 100% en aprender ABAP . Mi objetivo principal al estar aquí es conocer personas, armar una red de contactos y encontrar colegas con los que podamos apoyarnos mutuamente para crecer técnicamente, resolver dudas y hacer equipo (tanto ahora en la etapa de aprendizaje, como más adelante en el ámbito profesional) .

¿Qué estás aprendiendo o qué te genera curiosidad en este momento?
Me estoy preparando fuertemente con los manuales oficiales TAW10 y TAW12. Para mantener mi perfil al día con las nuevas tecnologías, también estoy tomando el curso de ABAP Cloud. Sin embargo, mi mayor curiosidad y hacia donde quiero dirigir mi carrera a futuro es a la integración de Inteligencia Artificial y Ciencia de Datos dentro del ecosistema SAP .

¿Algún interés fuera de SAP?
¡Totalmente! Me apasiona el ecosistema de los datos y la inteligencia artificial. De hecho, pronto, como parte de mi carrera en ESCOM, estaré estudiando a fondo Machine Learning, Procesamiento del Lenguaje Natural (NLP) y Minería de Datos. Mi meta es combinar estas áreas técnicas de vanguardia con el mundo de la consultoría, ya que me motiva muchísimo analizar cómo la ingeniería de software y los modelos predictivos pueden resolver problemas reales de negocio .

entry.title: Inconsistency between ACDOCA and ANLC (AR01) after Manual Revaluation with XMANPROPVAL_PN
entry.content:
Hi ,

I have a reconciliation issue in S/4HANA where the Asset Balance report (AR01) and ANLC table do not match the G/L (F.08/ACDOCA).

The issue:  We performed a manual revaluation in 2024 using XMANPROPVAL_PN to specify manual proportional accumulated depreciation.

Observation:  - In ACDOCA, the amounts are exactly as entered manually, and the Asset Number (ANLN1) is populated for all lines.

  • In AR01 and ANLC, the values for accumulated depreciation are different.

  • It seems the system is ignoring the manual proportional value from ACDOCA and instead performing its own proportional calculation when aggregating data for the Asset Subledger.

Key point: The difference is not the total revaluation, but a discrepancy in how the proportional part is calculated/displayed in the subledger vs. GL.

 Is there a way to force the Asset Subledger to use the manual values from ACDOCA instead of recalculating them? Are there any known SAP Notes for this "proportional mismatch" in S/4HANA?

entry.title: Resolving Missing HDI Build Plugin in Multi-Tenant SAP HANA Deployment
entry.content:
In a multi-tenant SAP HANA Cloud application using HDI containers for tenant isolation, the error "Could not find the 'com.sap.hana.di.schedulerjob' build plugin" occurs when new release artifacts require additional DI (Data Integration) plugins not present in consumer tenant containers.[1][2][3]

Scenario
Your production multi-tenant application serves live consumer tenants, each isolated in dedicated HDI containers for security and data segregation. A recent release introduced new HANA artifacts (e.g., tables, views) needing extra build plugins like `com.sap.hana.di.schedulerjob` for scheduler job support during HDI deployment or updates via tools like `cds deploy --to hana`.

Root Cause
Consumer tenant HDI containers lack the required DI plugins and admin privileges for library configuration, blocking Retriever deployments that trigger database updates. Standard CAP multitenancy provisions containers but doesn't auto-configure release-specific plugins across all tenants.

Resolution Steps
1. Grant Script Execution Rights: As database admin (e.g., SYSTEM user), assign `EXECUTE` privileges on target consumer containers to a dedicated script user (e.g., `<NEW_CONTAINER_ADMIN>`):
```
GRANT EXECUTE ON <container_name> TO <NEW_CONTAINER_ADMIN>;
```

2. Execute Privilege and Library Setup Scripts (connect as `<NEW_CONTAINER_ADMIN>` to the target container):
```sql
CREATE LOCAL TEMPORARY COLUMN TABLE #PRIVILEGES LIKE _SYS_DI.TT_API_PRIVILEGES;
INSERT INTO #PRIVILEGES (PRINCIPAL_NAME, PRIVILEGE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME)
SELECT '<NEW_CONTAINER_ADMIN>', PRIVILEGE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME
FROM _SYS_DI.T_DEFAULT_CONTAINER_ROLE_ADMIN_PRIVILEGES;
CALL <container_name>#DI.GRANT_CONTAINER_API_PRIVILEGES(#PRIVILEGES, _SYS_DI.T_NO_PARAMETERS, ?, ?, ?);
DROP TABLE #PRIVILEGES;
CALL <container_name>#DI.CONFIGURE_LIBRARIES(_SYS_DI.T_DEFAULT_LIBRARIES, _SYS_DI.T_NO_PARAMETERS, ?, ?, ?);
```
- The `?` placeholders capture output parameters (e.g., success flags, error details) – bind variables like `VARCHAR(100)` for each.
- `_SYS_DI.T_DEFAULT_CONTAINER_ROLE_ADMIN_PRIVILEGES` provides standard admin privileges.
- `_SYS_DI.T_DEFAULT_LIBRARIES` enables core plugins including schedulerjob for build-time artifact deployment.[3]

3. Trigger Retriever Deployment: Run your update process (e.g., `cds deploy` or CI/CD pipeline) to apply new artifacts. Tenant containers now support the missing plugin.

Key Learnings for Blog
- Proactive Plugin Check: Before releases, verify required DI plugins via `CALL <container>#DI.GET_CONTAINER_INFO()` and document in CI/CD.
- Automation Opportunity: Integrate these scripts into SaaS provisioning callbacks (e.g., via SAP Service Manager) for on-subscribe upgrades.
- Best Practice: Use separate HDI containers per tenant; reuse HANA instances across apps but isolate via Service Manager instances to avoid data loss on unsubscription.
- Monitoring: Post-fix, confirm via `_SYS_DI.T_CONTAINER_LIBRARIES` that `com.sap.hana.di.schedulerjob` is active.

This manual fix ensures zero-downtime updates in live multi-tenant environments while maintaining isolation. Test in dev tenants first.

entry.title: Re: is it possible to delete a sap user from 000 client b...
entry.content:
Officially, it's not possible to delete a sap user from one client by logging in from another client.

You must log in the client where you want to delete a user.

SAP users are client-dependent.

entry.title: Re: Migrating from ASANWEE to ASAAEMEE: From SAP Event Mesh to SAP Advanced Event Mesh
entry.content:
Hi @MustafaBensan 

Thank you for checking the SAP Product Availability Matrix and for raising this question.

The dates you referenced (End of Mainstream Maintenance: 31.12.2027 and End of Extended Maintenance: 31.12.2030) are currently displayed in the PAM for the technical software component versions. However, these dates should not be interpreted as the final availability or support end of the add-ons themselves.

Both the SAP NetWeaver Add-on for Event Enablement (ASANWEE) and the SAP Event Add-on for ERP (ASAAEMEE) will definitly be available beyond 31.12.2030. An adjustment of the maintenance information is in progress to avoid misunderstandings.

Thank you again for pointing this out.

entry.title: Re: Change customer in a professional services project
entry.content:
Good day @mleirvaag 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, the reason is, the customer in a professional services project is a key commercial detail the system uses to determine logics like sales order for billing, to determine the partner, pricing and depending on your set up, the revenue related processing. Because of those reasons, it is protected after you create a project to ensure documents are kept consistent even if your project is still in the planning phase.

Instead of changing the customer, the best practice is to:

  1. Create a new project with the correct customer
  2. Copy or reuse project structure (Work packages/WBS/task depending on your process) where you can.
  3. Cancel or close the incorrect project if no posting should continue there.

 

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/1504f6963f7b400789ef9b2ea2540a5f.html

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Using Adapter HTTP - Response 403 for OData API Post Method- How to solve the big challenge
entry.content:
Connecting SAP S/4 HANA or ERP systems with external applications using OData services is becoming the backbone of modern enterprise integrations. Tools such as SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) or Integration Suite’s using HTTP Adapter make it easy to consume or expose APIs—until you hit a frustrating roadblock:

“HTTP 403 – Forbidden” when calling an OData API POST method.

This error is notoriously deceptive. Your GET requests work fine. Your endpoint is correct. Your payload is correct. Yet POST still fails.
Why?

In this blog, we’ll break down why HTTP 403 happens, what makes POST requests special, and—most importantly—how to solve this challenge with proven, real-world fixes.

1. Understanding the 403 Forbidden Error

A 403 Forbidden response means:

The server understood your request but refuses to authorize it.

In the context of OData POST calls via the HTTP adapter, this almost always indicates an authorization, CSRF, or policy-related issue.

2. Why GET Works but POST Fails

Many developers test an OData service with a GET call, find that it works, and assume the setup is correct. But POST behaves differently because it:

  • Modifies data, triggering stricter authorization checks
  • Requires CSRF token handling (depending on the endpoint)
  • May require additional headers

This is why GET succeeding does not prove the integration is fully configured.

3. The Top Causes of HTTP 403 for OData POST (and How to Fix Them)

Cause 1 — Missing or Incorrect CSRF Token

Many SAP OData services require a valid X-CSRF-Token for POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE.

✔ Solution:
Perform a GET request with header:

X-CSRF-Token: Fetch

and capture:

  • Returned CSRF token
  • Returned cookies

Then send them along in your POST call:

X-CSRF-Token: <token>
Cookie: <cookies>

Cause 2 — Incorrect or Missing Headers

Some OData services enforce additional constraints.

Mandatory headers may include:

Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json

4. How to Troubleshoot 403 Efficiently

Step 1 — Test POST via Postman

  • Fetch CSRF token first
  • Send cookies + token in POST
  • Add all required headers

Step 2 — Check SAP Gateway Error Logs

Use transaction /IWFND/ERROR_LOG or /IWBEP/ERROR_LOG.

You may see:

  • "No CREATE authorization"
  • "CSRF Token validation failed"
  • "Request method not allowed"

These messages are extremely helpful.

5. Example Adapter HTTP Configuration for POST (CPI)

Headers 

Method: POST
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
X-CSRF-Token: <fetched token>
Cookie: <SAP cookies>
Body (Example)
{
  "Name": "Sample Item",
  "Quantity": 10
}

Set Header for Cookie + CSRF token 

 Groovy Script 1(Shown from above Iflow image)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;


def Message processData(Message message) {
    def headers = message.getHeaders();
    def cookie = headers.get("Set-Cookie");
    def csrf = headers.get("x-csrf-token");
    StringBuffer bufferedCookie = new StringBuffer();
    for (Object item : cookie)
    {
        bufferedCookie.append(item + "; ");
    }
    message.setHeader("Cookie", bufferedCookie.toString());
    message.setHeader("x-csrf-token", csrf)

    return message
}

POST Method  

6. Conclusion – Turning a Frustrating 403 into a Successful POST

The HTTP 403 Forbidden error for OData POST calls is one of the most common yet misunderstood issues when integrating SAP systems. But once you understand:

  • CSRF handling
  • Role/authorization requirements
  • Header and policy rules
  • Gateway restrictions

…the problem becomes easy to solve.

Following the troubleshooting guidelines above will help you quickly identify the root cause and ensure your POST requests work reliably—whether you're integrating via CPI, API Management, BTP, or direct HTTP calls.

entry.title: Budget Management in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Folks,

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Budget Management (known as Funds Management in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud) has been revamped and its structure has been changed. Following is a brief introduction to Budget Management in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Introduction to Budget Management in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

Budget management supports budgeting-based management of funds at public sector institutions where revenue and expenditure are budgeted according to legislative criteria, such as payment in kind, area of responsibility, program/function and source of funds. All the financial transactions which leads to eventual outflow of cash are monitored strictly via an allocated budget and revenues planning and monitoring is also done. Planning, posting, maintenance of budget, monitoring is all possible if we have Budget Management in place .

The business benefits of using Budget Management are as follows:

  • Maintain and monitor the allocation, distribution, or changes of approved budgets
  • Plan, standardize, schedule, and monitor financial and budget close activities
  • Automate and streamline budget processes, and reduce manual effort
  • Optimize closing of open commitments and financial and managerial accounting
  • Immediate budget availability checking to ensure regulatory compliance
  • Real-time reporting to aid in time-sensitive managerial decision

A helpful link is given below which has helped me a lot to demystify the Budget management and its underlying concepts in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, I am still learning it and will keep you posted if I found something new.

Describing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Public Sector

Scope Item for PSM-Budget Management:

Following scope item was automatically activated when I selected the Public Sector Management.

PSM Bundle Activated

 

 

 

 

When we activate Public Sector Management in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud , it automatically setup and configure SAP Budget Management (known as PSM-Funds Management in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud) , SAP Grants Management and Fund Accounting.

Budget Management in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

In this knowledge article, I will shed some light on my experience with execution and setting up Budget Management in Public cloud. There are lot things which have been set to work differently in SAP Budget Management. If the public sector budget management solution is activated, transactions impacting the budget require budget-relevant account assignments. Often the budget-relevant account assignments are not manually entered, they are derived by the system.

Key Differences between SAP S/4HANA Private and Public Cloud from Budgeting Perspective:

Budget management reuses the master data from other SAP components in combination with PSM-specific master data as account assignments for budget-relevant transaction postings. I will illustrate this statement with below table. 

I also could not see any option to post payment and commitment budget separately in Budget Management, if anyone has come across such functionality in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud then please do let me know.

SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

In SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud or even in ECC, for Funds Management system to work, two FM account assignments were mandatory namely Commitment Items and Fund Center

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Budget management works with a mandatory combination of Fund , Budget Period, Cost Center & Account Number

In SAP S/4HANA Private cloud, there could be one to one, one to many or many to one relationship between Commitment Item to General Ledger, you could automatically derive Commitment item if there was 1:1 relationship .

In SAP S/4HANA Public cloud is has flipped altogether. You can only have 1:1 relationship between your General ledger and Budget Account . The same concept is applicable for cost center.

In SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, the department for which budget was posted was known as Fund Center.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Fund center has been replaced with Cost Center as it has reused the cost accounting assignment.

In SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, the budget head for which budget was posted in combination with Fund center was known as Commitment item.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, 

the G/L account is used as master data for the budget account.

Use of Fund was not mandatory.

Use of Fund is mandatory for posting the budget, the type of fund external or internal plays a critical role here.

Budget period was optional in PSM-FM and you had to activate it to be used.

Budget period is mandatory in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

WBS never existed in PSM-FM

We can optionally use WBS element in Budget management to post Budget.

Use of grant account assignment was only needed in PSM-FM Budgeting workbench when SAP GM-GTE was activated.

Use of Grant account assignment is mandatory in Budget Management. 

Funded Program was available in PSM-FM budgeting and derivation rule.

Funded Program does not exist in Budget management.

Important Master Data Object in Budget Management:

Following are the important master data objects in Budget management with their pictorial overview.

Fund Master Data:

Fund master data is an important master data object in budget management without which Budget can not be posted. It is maintained by app SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Budget Account Master Data:

FIORI App which is used to create budget account which is nothing but a General Ledger Account is SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Budget Period:

Budget Period master data is managed via FIORI App Manage Budget period. The budget period master data is pre-delivered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud , Budget periods have been maintained for the entire FY e.g. FY 2026 is represented by one budget period, you may create your own master data for Budget period, if you want to maintain and post budget month wise.

 

I will not go into the details of other master data objects in PSM-Budget Management like Cost Center and WBS as they are self explanatory .

Derivation Rule in S/4HANA Public Cloud:

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, derivation rules are maintained very differently unlike Private Cloud where we had FMDERIVER/FMDERIVE to maintain derivation rules, we have a dedicated FIORI App to Maintain Substitution/Validation Rules for Public Sector as shown below.

 

Posting Budget in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

Budgeting is a holistic process and it follows many critical steps like preparing, planning, consumption monitoring, returns and supplements etc. It has been described in below flow diagram which has been taken from SAP help library.

In Budget management, budget is posted via following app. 

There are lot of functionalities within above app and it would require me to write a separate blog on the future and processes available within Budget posting app. For now, please just bear in that the original budget, supplement, transfers or return etc. happens using this app.

Budget Management Integration with SAP Analytical Cloud (SAC):

The SAP_COM_0761 communication scenario offers the integration of Public Sector Management (PSM) on SAP SAP S/4HANA Cloud as a data provider with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).

This scenario offers OData services for the following:

  • Fund and hierarchy
  • Grant and hierarchy
  • Budget period and hierarchy
  • Actuals and Commitment

The following figure provides an overview of how to do the following:

  • The master data, budget, and actuals and commitment are transferred between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • The budget is prepared in SAP Analytics Cloud

Recording Commitments/Actuals:

In public sector organisations, no business activity that will result in a future payment can be approved without an available budget. Therefore, it is crucial to reserve budget funds as early as possible to ensure proper financial planning. When governments anticipate future payments, typically due to an operational commitment document, they must formally set aside the required budget. This reservation process, known as "commitment updating," is documented via a financial budget consumption record and is subject to active availability checks.

In some instances, a formal commitment document may not exist, meaning the actual expenditure directly consumes the budget. Both scenarios – whether the spending is linked to a prior commitment or directly consumes the budget – are referred to as "actual updating." The combination of commitment and actual postings constitutes the total budget consumption.

Public Sector Management (PSM)-specific information for both commitment and actual transactions is recorded in the universal journal (table ACDOCA). This involves adding the relevant PSM account assignments, master data, and budgetary attributes to the universal journal entries when documents are posted. Specifically, commitment postings (such as purchase requisitions, purchase orders, or earmarked funds) update ledger 0E, while actual expenditure documents update ledger 0L.

You can learn a lot more about updating commitment/actuals using below link 
Recording Commitments and Actuals

Recording Commitments:

We will create a PO to record commitment of 5000 USD on budget dimensions for which I have posted budget. Commitment update logic that I have observed in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud PSM conforms to PBET (Period Based Encumbrance Tracking) . If you want to learn more about it then please read my blog on PBET
Period Based Encumbrance Tracking in PSM-FM - SAP Community

Recording Actuals:

Posting invoices, expense JVs , GRNs impacts the actuals and converts commitments into actual postings. I have posted a GRN of 60% of PO value which was 5000 USD. I have posted 3000 USD as GRN and now we will see effect of recording actuals on reporting and commitments which were already recorded.

Manage AVC Exceptions:

There is a FIORI app to make exceptions for cost center or budget account if you want to exclude them from budget check. You can do it for a specific user and control address for a given date range and assign a tolerance profile which does not check budget etc.

Budget Management Integration:

You can learn about budget management integration with other components within SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system using below link. The link offers deep dive into integration of budget management with Finance, SuccessFactors, Procurement, Concur, Plant maintenance etc.

Describing Finance Integration

Finishing Lines:

In summary, while both SAP Budget Management in the Public Cloud and SAP Funds Management are powerful tools for financial control, they cater to distinct strategic imperatives. The former offers a modern, cloud-native approach aligned with future-proof digital transformation, while the latter provides established, comprehensive functionalities. Understanding these differences is paramount for organisations navigating their journey in the SAP ecosystem. Please let me know if there are improvements or corrections needed 😊.

entry.title: Re: Monitoring Project Costs with Project Financial Bookl...
entry.content:
Good day @djwafelap 

Thank you for your question.

What you described is a common confusion because Project Financial Booklet-Enteprise Projects does not behave like a generic line item report. It has specific scope and prerequisites, while apps like Projects Plan/Actual and line item apps are broader and show postings more directly.

See https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3680165

Here is a similar scenario: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/planned-revenue-in-project-financial-booklet-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public/qaq-p/14331443

The most probable issue you are facing is:

Your project does not meet the prerequisites for booklet app. The reason is it is intended for Enterprise projects, and some views especially margin and revenue related views require the project to be set up as a project with revenue.

The following reference https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3655022 points to this kind of behaviour.

You probably used the filters/columns that exclude the data which is driven by apps such Ledger, Company Code, Period, Projects with Revenue, etc. If one of these is not aligned, the project may appear, but values stay blank.

You may be comparing two apps with different purposes.

  • Project Plan/Actual and line item apps provide more direct visibility of posted amounts
  • Project Financial Booklet provides curated analytical view with scenario specific logic and prerequisites.

So, if you see data in one app and not in the booklet does not automatically mean posting is wrong.

Ensure that:

  • Your chosen project is an Enterprise Project and a Project with Revenue

  • The Ledger, Company Code and Fiscal year filters you chose match the ones in the booklet you posted.
  • Your booklet layout includes the (Actual Cost vs Recognised Cost) correct measure.
  • You have selected booklet page or filter variant is appropriate for your project scenario

To summarise:

  • The behaviour you described is due to app scope and prerequisites, not necessarily a posting error.
  • In Public Cloud, Project Financial Booklet- Enterprise Projects is an analytical app that provides scenario specific views, while Projects Plan and line item apps show the costs you have posted.

If your posting is visible in line items but not in the booklet, it is often a filter, layout or prerequisite issue rather than missing accounting data.

See the following reference for further details: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3596986

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Manage Supplier Line Items- Profit Center is now shwo...
entry.content:
Good day @Sandeep5060 

Thank you for your question.

What you described is a standard behaviour in Manage Supplier Line Items.

What you are seeing is two different situations:

  • In one case, Profit Center shows in line items
  • In another case, Profit Center can be added in the selection/filter, and the supplier line item has a profit center in the underlying accounting, but the profit Center column is blank in the app.

That happens because Manage Supplier Line Items can only display fields that have a one to one relationship to the supplier line item.

Profit Center is stored on the G/L line item level, not on the supplier line item/document header level. So if one supplier invoice has multiple G/L lines and different profit centers, then the app cannot show one single profit center value on the supplier line item row. This scenario is described here: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2901005 and https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3346815

That is why the filed may exist in filters but still not display reliably in the line item result for all documents.

I recommend you use Display Line Items in General Ledger for profit center analysis. The app shows the G/L line items where the Profit Center is actually stored.  This is described in the reference provided above.

See https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2901005 and https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3346815 for more details.

Here is a quick guide:

In your manage supplier line items app,

After executing you may notice some profit centers are blank, which describes

Alternatively, you can see the real Profit Center in accounting line items by using the Display Line Items in General Ledger. You can filter by Supplier, Company Code, Fiscal Year and optionally Journal Entry and execute.

Then display Profit Center, G/L Account etc and you will see Profit Center on the G/L lines

To summarise:

  • The behaviour you see is standard and not a configuration error.
  • Manage Supplier Line Items cannot always display Profit Center because it only shows fields with one-to-one relation to the supplier line item.
  • Profit Center is on G/L line items, so use Display Line Items in General Ledger for reliable reporting.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Different Posting Date and Tax Reporting Date Billing...

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Service Management [ESM] - How to enable Business Document
entry.content:
Previous Blog : How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End

Business Documents

Business Document feature available in SAP Enterprise Service Management SKU (ESM)

Business Documents represent back-end records in a semantic way and can create, update, or display those records. Values in the table can be populated automatically using AI services or entered manually.

Business documents can capture data from email message or case subject and description, attachments of various file types, or using manual data entry. Attachment data and entity information is extracted with generative AI using large language models.

You, as an administrator, can create business document schemas to represent data patterns for different scenarios. Schemas specify which data to use in your business documents and how you want the data formatted in your solution. If you've enabled business documents for a specific case type, you can find the Business Documents tab on the case detail view. Use conditions to make specific business document fields required, read only, or hidden.

Business Document Integration

Create an outbound configuration to enable actions to send or retrieve data from external systems, or to populate values in dropdown menus in business documents based on a dynamic data source.

Prerequisites

Set up an outbound communication system under System Settings -  Integration  - Communication Systems.

Procedure

Go to your user menu and choose System Settings  - Business Documents - Business Document Integration.

  • Choose + (Create).
  • Enter a name and select the communication system.
  • Choose Search and Add to add existing HTTP data outbound configurations or + (Create) to create a new configuration.

Create Business Documents

Select + Create Business Document, Enter the required information, and save

Your new business document appears in the Business Documents list with the header details on the right. A new draft version of the business document appears in the Versions table. Select + Actions to activate the version, copy to a new business document, create new version, or delete the version. There can only be one draft version. You can activate multiple versions but there must only be one active version within the specified validity period.

Select + in the Designer tab to add controls and fields to the business document table.

Add actions to the business document. Go to the Actions tab and select + Add Action.

Go to the Input Options tab to choose one or more input methods for your business document.

Configure Business Document Extraction Schemas

Use schemas to configure document extraction from attachments.

Add Business Document to Case Type

  1. Go to User Menu > System Settings  > All Settings  > Cases  > Case Types.
  2. Select a case type.
  3. Select  (Open in Detail View) to open the case type details.
  4. Switch on Business Documents in the Features Settings section

  1. Choose the Additional Features tab in the case type header.
  2. In the Business Documents section, choose the  (Search) icon to search for a business document to add to this case type.
  3. Choose + (Add) to add the business document to the case type.

    You can assign only one business document to each case type. When a case of this type is created, the Business Documents tab appears in the case detail view.

entry.title: Using AI Agent Cline to Remove Deprecated Code from SAP Fiori Applications
entry.content:
Using AI Agent Cline to Remove Deprecated Code from SAP Fiori Applications

 

Introduction

 

SAP Fiori applications are built on the robust foundation of UI5 libraries, which are updated each quarter to deliver new features and controls. Traditionally, these libraries have evolved with a strong emphasis on backward compatibility, where addressing deprecated code was encouraged but not strictly enforced.

However, with the imminent Fiori UI5 2.0 release, all deprecated elements will be removed, making the elimination of deprecated controls not just a recommendation, but a clear guideline.

In addition, maintaining clean and up-to-date code is crucial for enhancibility, performance, security, and compatibility. Deprecated APIs, outdated libraries, and legacy patterns can lead to technical debt and potential issues in future upgrades.

In this blog, we’ll explore how AI Agent Cline can help identify and remove deprecated code from Fiori applications efficiently.

 

1. Configuring Cline for Fiori Applications

Before using Cline, ensure it is properly configured to analyse SAPUI5/Fiori applications.

Prerequisites

  1. GitHub Copilot license
  2. Visual Studio Code
  3. Installation of GitHub Copilot extension in Visual Studio Code

 

Installation of Cline extension in Visual Studio Code

  • Click on Extension icon (Ctrl+Shift+X)
  • Search for cline
  • Click on install
  • Open Cline by clicking on the Cline icon in the vertical icon toolbar in VS code.

  • The Cline welcome screen is shown.

  • Click on Settings icon in the top right corner

  •  Select GitHub Copilot as the API Provider and copilot -claude-sonnet-4.5 as Language Model and press “Done”.

 

Installation of the MCP server for SAP Fiori

 

  • In the cline panel, click on the MCP Servers icon on the top-right corner.

  • Click on Configure
  • Click on Configure MCP Servers.
  • The file cline_mcp_settings.json opens. Maintain UI5 MCP server as shown below and click on Done.

Note: (@ui5/mcp-server) is a SAP published UI5 MCP server. It allows AI assistants to write SAPUI5 code. SAP currently prevents the usage of MCP servers together with GitHub Copilot. Hence, Cline is being used as an alternative. However a GitHub Copilot license is required for accessing SAP’s approved AI models. UI5 MCP server supports UI5 Linter along with other tools. Linter in particular is useful in identifying deprecated code from the applications and for working on subsequent fixes.

Now that we have configured VS code with all the required tools, we can proceed with fixing the deprecated code issues.

 

2. Prompting Cline to Evaluate Deprecated Code

 

After trying multiple approaches to prompt Cline to evaluate and fix deprecated code following steps proved to give consistent results. This approach is equally effective for free style applications as well as Fiori element based applications.

Tips:

  1. Following example is an approach which worked for my use case. There were apps with ~800 issues. So going slow initially helped me gain confidence in the solution provided by AI. There by reducing subsequent effort in error fixing.
  2. In Cline, describe the task as precisely as possible.
  3. Clone the repository to your workspace
  4. Mention the relevant project in task prompt, if you have multiple repos in your workspace.
  5. Mention that the available MCP servers should be used.
  6. Working context is set in the first prompt and then tasks are executed under it. If a new task is started, then the context is to be set again.
  7. Cline would ask for your approval for using MCP server, for executing certain commands. Auto approval can be configured in MCP configuration.
  8. File changes by Cline can be reviewed in Git Lens view of VS code.
  9. If automation test scripts with good coverage is available in the repository, a better approach will be to fix the deprecated code in test scripts first. These scripts can then be regularly executed by cline to validate changes.

Example:

 

Prompt 1: Setting “Working context”

Cline was asked to find and evaluate deprecated code issues (both error and warning) in the application using linter command. Working context is set for Cline to follow SAP UI5 guidelines only and use the configured UI5 MCP server. This context helps Cline to stay within the SAPUI5 guidelines while solving the issues going forward.

 

Cline would eventually categorize the issues and prepare a roadmap for fixing the same.

In cases with large number of identified issued, which might span over days to fix, Cline might prepare a .md file with following type of details for future reference.v                 

  • Categorisation of the issues into Low Medium and High
  • Issue detail with suggested solution
  • Number of files affected with same issue
  • Effort breakup to fix the issues

  • Testing strategy to mitigate subsequent breakdowns
  • Resource required

  • Risk Assessment

 

Prompt 2: Start Fixing the Issues

 

For start we picked 1 type of issue, and fixed it in all files. Maybe start with a low or medium category. Such issues require less effort, get fixed quickly with negligible breakdown in the application. The number of issues start reducing at a fast pace.

We asked Cline to suggest which issue to start with. It evaluated the list of issues to prepare a suggestion. Developer can choose the fixing strategy and include it in the prompt for Cline to suggest accordingly.

We started with a low priority issue as per suggestion shared by Cline.

For first few files, Cline asked developer to validate the changes before saving. Cline can be instructed to save the file directly.

Once some issues are fixed and tested, you can instruct cline to replicate the fix in other files without approval. Change log can then be viewed in Git Lens.

A summary get generated by Cline for developer to check how far they have come along with the suggestion for the next steps.

Note:

  1. We were able to resolve 200 (low + medium) errors and 200 warnings in 1 PD using this approach.
  2. If your application has custom logic coded in UI. Recommendation would be to break a big controller task into small steps for Cline. This would improve the efficiency of the solution being generated.
  3. Considerable effort reduction can be achieved once initial steps are eased into.
  4. Approximately 30-40% of manual effort is saved with the approach suggested. Once the confidence in AI suggestions improve, the number might be higher.

Tags: #SAPUI5 #Fiori #DeprecatedCode #AI #Cline #SAPDevelopment

entry.title: When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship
entry.content:
When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship

I have developed a comprehensive playbook demonstrating how modern SAP tools and AI can deliver solutions in DAYS instead of MONTHS.

The shocking math:
- Traditional approach: 6 months, $650K in delayed value
- SAP tools + AI: 1 week, $25K in delayed value

This isn't just theory—it's a practical guide that covers:
- CDS Views for data modeling
- CAP for service generation
- Fiori Elements for instant UIs
- Integration Suite for connectivity
- AI to accelerate everything

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/rajesh-putumbaka-456267101_sap-btp-ai-development-playbook-ugcPost-7431029390173696001-y7Yn?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAABnYP8IBvlY4GDRmR8utVFLHXZ61gUvSa-0
